cases.title: The Lethargic Toddler
cases.presentation: A 2-year-old is found unusually quiet and difficult to arouse by a caregiver. No fever is reported. A glucose meter is available on the unit.
cases.question: What is the appropriate next step?
options: A: Transport without further assessment since no fever is present.
B: Check the child's blood glucose, since hypoglycemia is a common and rapidly reversible cause of altered mental status.
C: Assume the child is simply tired and reassess in 30 minutes.
D: Withhold any intervention until arrival at the hospital.
cases.explanation: Any altered child should have a glucose check when a meter is available, since hypoglycemia is common in children and rapidly reversible. This should be done promptly rather than assuming a benign cause without checking.
cases.clinicalPearl: Glucose check first, every time, when altered mental status is present and a meter is available — it's fast, easy, and can immediately explain (and treat) the problem.

cases.title: Seizure at the Six-Minute Mark
cases.presentation: A 3-year-old has been having generalized tonic-clonic seizure activity for 6 minutes with no sign of stopping. The caregiver reports no fever and this has never happened before.
cases.question: What does this presentation represent, and what is the priority?
options: A: A simple febrile seizure requiring only observation.
B: Status epilepticus — a time-critical emergency requiring rapid escalation of care and transport.
C: A normal pediatric seizure pattern requiring no escalation.
D: This should be timed for another 10 minutes before taking any action.
cases.explanation: A seizure lasting more than 5 minutes meets the definition of status epilepticus, a time-critical emergency. This is not a simple febrile seizure (there is no fever, and duration alone already exceeds the definition), and requires immediate escalation of care; benzodiazepine dosing is addressed in the PALS reviewer.
cases.clinicalPearl: Five minutes is the line — once a seizure crosses it, treat it as status epilepticus and escalate, don't keep waiting.

cases.title: Bulging Fontanelle After a Fall
cases.presentation: A 5-month-old fell from a changing table an hour ago. The infant is increasingly difficult to arouse, has a bulging fontanelle, and now has an irregular breathing pattern.
cases.question: What should this presentation prompt, and what should be avoided?
options: A: Suspicion for increased intracranial pressure — avoid hypoxia and hypotension, position to avoid jugular compression, and transport rapidly.
B: No specific concern since infants commonly have bulging fontanelles.
C: Aggressive fluid restriction and permissive hypoxia.
D: Delay transport until the fontanelle returns to normal.
cases.explanation: A bulging fontanelle with decreasing responsiveness and an irregular respiratory pattern after a fall suggests increased intracranial pressure. Field priorities are avoiding hypoxia and hypotension (both worsen secondary brain injury), positioning to avoid jugular venous compression, and rapid transport.
cases.clinicalPearl: After head trauma, a bulging fontanelle plus a change in responsiveness is not incidental — treat it as rising intracranial pressure and protect the brain from secondary injury.
